responses.text: In 2019, Yale University researchers developed a system called BrainEx that partially restored some cellular functions in pig brains hours after the animals were killed.
The scientists obtained 32 pig heads from a food processing plant, meaning the animals were already slaughtered for meat, not killed for the experiment.
Starting four hours after death, the researchers hooked the isolated brains to the BrainEx device, which pumped a synthetic, blood-like chemical solution through the neural tissue.
This process successfully slowed cell death, preserved tissue structure, and restarted some metabolic capabilities such as the consumption of oxygen and glucose.
However, the researchers stressed that the brains did not regain organized electrical activity normally associated with consciousness, awareness, or perception.
The BrainEx solution intentionally included chemical compounds to block neuronal activity, both to give the cells a better chance at recovery and to prevent any possibility of the brains "waking up."
Despite a lack of large-scale, coordinated activity, tests on slices of hippocampal tissue confirmed that individual neurons remained capable of firing electrical signals.
The results challenged the long-held assumption that oxygen deprivation leads to a rapid, irreversible cascade of total cell death in the mammalian brain.

While the achievement sparked bioethical discussions about animal research protections and the clinical definition of brain death, scientists generally agree the study did not functionally resurrect or awaken the brains.
